title: UK Borrows Less Than Expected in June, But Year-to-Date Outlook Darkens
dek: UK government borrowing in June 2026 hit 16 billion pounds, 7.9 billion below the same month last year and slightly undershooting the Office for Budget Responsibility's forecast of 16.3 billion. Yet year-to-date borrowing has run 2.7 billion pounds above OBR projections, signaling structural fiscal strain ahead of the autumn Budget.
author: The GoCheckMyNews Desk
key_fact: June borrowing came in at 16 billion pounds, beating forecast, but year-to-date borrowing is 2.7 billion pounds above OBR projections, constraining room for new spending.
bottom_line: June's better-than-expected borrowing figure masks a year-to-date overshoot that economists say leaves little room for the new government to fund major policy changes without breaching or creatively reinterpreting its inherited fiscal rules. Watch the autumn Budget in the coming weeks to see how Burnham and Healey resolve the gap between campaign promises and the fiscal constraints they claim to accept; any loosening of the rules or dramatic revision of OBR forecasts would test their credibility argument.
body: UK government borrowing in June 2026 fell to 16 billion pounds, approximately 7.9 billion pounds lower than June 2025 and slightly below the Office for Budget Responsibility's forecast of 16.3 billion pounds. The better-than-expected monthly figure briefly buoyed markets. But a sharper measure of the government's fiscal health tells a more complicated story: year-to-date borrowing has reached 57.6 billion pounds, down 3.7 billion pounds from the same period last year, yet running 2.7 billion pounds ahead of the OBR's full-year projection. That overshoot constrains the room for policy maneuver the new Prime Minister and Chancellor claim to have found within inherited fiscal rules.

Public sector net debt stands at nearly 3 trillion pounds, a figure the Office for National Statistics describes as close to the annual value of all goods and services produced in the UK. Under the public sector net financial liabilities measure adopted in the October 2024 Budget, total debt reached 2.7 trillion pounds at the end of June 2026, equivalent to 84.5 percent of GDP. The government paid 11.8 billion pounds in debt interest payments in June, nearly a third lower than the same month last year but still the fourth highest June figure on record. Those twin pressures, mounting debt and persistent interest servicing costs, define the fiscal constraint Burnham and Healey now navigate.

Labor markets show signs of cooling. The unemployment rate remained unchanged at 4.9 percent. Regular earnings, excluding bonuses, rose at an annual pace of 3.4 percent in the March to May period. But private sector wage growth, according to BBC reporting, fell below 3 percent for the first time since 2020, a slowdown economists say may prompt the Bank of England to hold interest rates steady at 3.75 percent when it meets next week. Yael Selfin, chief economist at KPMG, said the subdued wage growth made it more likely the central bank would hold rates, a move that could ease borrowing pressure on the government in the near term but signals broader economic deceleration.

Yield on 10-year government bonds climbed above 5 percent, reaching 5.03 percent late Monday afternoon and slipping back to 5.01 percent Tuesday. The rise reflects market concern about the government's debt trajectory and the room available for new spending commitments.

The new leadership announced a VAT cut on household electricity bills from 5 percent to zero beginning October, funded by savings from cancellation of the digital ID program. Chancellor John Healey said in a statement that fiscal credibility is the bedrock for economic stability and national security. Burnham and Healey have pledged to stick to former Chancellor Rachel Reeves' fiscal rules on spending and borrowing, though Burnham said he would use any flexibility within them to help with policy changes. But Labour MP Darren Jones, sacked as chief secretary to the Prime Minister on Monday, disputed that characterization. He accused the government of announcing an unfunded tax cut in the VAT reduction on electricity.

Economists offered mixed reads. Ruth Gregory, deputy chief UK economist at Capital Economics, called June's figure a rare piece of good news but cautioned that the public finances are fragile and there is limited scope for extra borrowing. James Smith, chief UK economist at ING, said year-to-date borrowing running ahead of OBR projections was a reminder of the challenges the new Chancellor and Prime Minister face, one that signaled a difficult picture at the autumn Budget with lots of tough choices to be made.
